h1Title: Ankylosing Spondylitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis & Treatment
heroOverview: Ankylosing spondylitis (AS) is a chronic inflammatory arthritis primarily targeting the sacroiliac joints and spine, characterised by inflammatory back pain that improves with activity but worsens with rest. Approximately 0.1-0.5% of the global population is affected, with a strong genetic association with the HLA-B27 antigen present in 90-95% of AS patients in European populations. Men are diagnosed two to three times more frequently than women, though women commonly experience diagnostic delays because their imaging changes are subtler. Without treatment, progressive spinal fusion (ankylosis) producing the radiographic 'bamboo spine' causes irreversible loss of mobility in 20-30% of patients over 20 years. Tumour necrosis factor (TNF) inhibitors and IL-17 inhibitors introduced over the past 20 years dramatically reduce inflammation and slow structural progression.

keyStats.prevalence: 0.1-0.5% of the general population worldwide; approximately 1.4 million people in the United States (Sieper et al. 2015)
keyStats.demographics: Men diagnosed 2-3× more often than women; HLA-B27 positive in 90-95% of European AS patients; onset typically ages 20-30 years
keyStats.avgAge: Symptom onset median age 24-26 years; diagnostic delay of 7-10 years from symptom onset remains common
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 6-9 million people globally; highest prevalence in populations with high HLA-B27 frequency (Arctic populations up to 1-2%)

definition: Ankylosing spondylitis (ICD-10: M45) is the prototype of the axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) spectrum, a group of inflammatory arthritides sharing HLA-B27 association, enthesitis (inflammation at tendon/ligament insertion points), sacroiliitis, and overlap with psoriasis, inflammatory bowel disease, and uveitis. AS is defined by the presence of definite radiographic sacroiliitis (modified New York criteria: grade ≥2 bilateral or grade ≥3 unilateral) combined with at least one clinical feature (inflammatory back pain, restriction of lumbar motion, or restricted chest expansion). Pathologically, AS involves immune-mediated enthesitis at spinal facet joints and the fibrocartilaginous sacroiliac joints, followed by aberrant bone formation and eventual ossification of spinal ligaments and annulus fibrosus, producing syndesmophytes and fusion. The 2009 Assessment of SpondyloArthritis International Society (ASAS) classification extended the axSpA concept to include non-radiographic axSpA (nr-axSpA), where MRI-detected sacroiliac inflammation fulfils criteria before bone changes are evident on X-ray. AS and nr-axSpA together constitute the axSpA spectrum managed along the same treatment pathway. Biologics, NSAIDs, and intensive physiotherapy are the cornerstones of management, led by rheumatologists.

typesClassification.description: Definite sacroiliitis on plain X-ray per modified New York criteria (grade ≥2 bilateral or grade ≥3-4 unilateral SI joint changes), combined with inflammatory back pain, restricted lumbar motion, or reduced chest expansion. Represents established structural disease.

Sacroiliac joint inflammation on MRI (bone marrow oedema on STIR sequences) or positive HLA-B27 with ≥2 SpA features, without definite X-ray sacroiliitis. Biologically identical to AS; responds to TNF inhibitors; may progress to radiographic AS over 2-10 years in 10-30% of patients.

Asymmetrical oligoarthritis of large joints (hip, knee, shoulder), enthesitis (Achilles tendon, plantar fascia, tibial tuberosity), and dactylitis (sausage digit) occur in 30-40% of AS patients, widening the clinical picture beyond the spine.

Acute anterior uveitis occurs in 25-40% of AS patients (the most common extra-articular feature); psoriasis in 10-25%; inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn's or ulcerative colitis) in 5-10%; aortic regurgitation and cardiac conduction defects in long-standing disease.

primarySymptoms: Chronic inflammatory low back pain with insidious onset before age 45, lasting more than 3 months, worst in the morning and after rest, and improving significantly with exercise or movement — the ASAS definition of inflammatory back pain.
Morning stiffness of the spine lasting more than 30 minutes, typically 1-2 hours in active disease, easing with hot shower and gentle movement.
Sacroiliac joint pain causing deep buttock pain that may alternate sides in early disease, often radiating to the posterior thigh but not below the knee.
Progressive loss of lumbar lordosis and spinal flexion, measurable by the Schober test — a reduction in the normal 5 cm increase in lumbosacral distance with maximal forward flexion.
Reduced chest expansion below 2.5 cm in active disease, reflecting involvement of costovertebral joints and reducing respiratory reserve.
Acute anterior uveitis — sudden unilateral red eye, photophobia, and blurred vision requiring urgent ophthalmological assessment; occurs in approximately 30-40% of AS patients.
Enthesitis — pain and tenderness at the Achilles tendon insertion, plantar fascia origin, tibial tuberosity, or iliac crest, reflecting the underlying entheseal pathology of spondyloarthritis.
Hip arthritis causing groin pain, limited internal rotation, and flexion contracture — present in 20-35% of AS patients and the most disabling peripheral joint involvement.
Fatigue disproportionate to other symptoms — a frequently reported and often under-recognised feature of active AS, correlating with disease activity and sleep disturbance.
Gradual flattening of the normal cervical and lumbar curves and hyperkyphosis (forward stoop) in long-standing disease, progressively restricting upright posture, vision ahead, and swallowing.
earlyWarningSigns: Inflammatory back pain beginning before age 45, present for more than 3 months, worse in the second half of the night with early morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes — this triad warrants referral for rheumatological assessment.
Unexplained acute anterior uveitis in a person under 45 — approximately 25-40% of these patients have underlying axial spondyloarthritis.
Achilles tendinopathy or plantar fasciitis not responding to standard physiotherapy in a young adult — enthesitis is often the presenting feature before spinal symptoms develop.
First-degree relative with known AS or HLA-B27 positivity in a young adult with new back pain — familial risk is approximately 10-fold above the general population.
emergencySigns: Sudden neurological deficits (leg weakness, bladder or bowel dysfunction) in an AS patient — cauda equina syndrome from arachnoid diverticula or atlantoaxial subluxation requires emergency MRI and surgical assessment.

New-onset severe back pain after minor trauma in a patient with known bamboo spine — spinal fracture through fused segments is highly unstable and carries a high risk of cord injury; immediate CT spine required.
Acute anterior uveitis with severe pain and visual loss not responding to topical steroids within 48 hours — requires same-day ophthalmology review to prevent sight loss.
Dyspnoea and severe hypoxia in a long-standing AS patient — apical pulmonary fibrosis and restricted chest expansion can cause progressive respiratory failure requiring urgent pulmonary assessment.

causes.description: HLA-B27 is present in 90-95% of European AS patients versus 8% of the general European population. HLA-B27 modulates antigen presentation to CD8+ T cells and may present arthritogenic peptides from enteric bacteria that trigger sacroiliac inflammation. Risk in HLA-B27-positive first-degree relatives of an AS patient is approximately 15-20%.

Beyond HLA-B27, the endoplasmic reticulum aminopeptidase 1 (ERAP1) gene — which trims peptides for HLA presentation — is a major AS risk locus. Polymorphisms in genes of the IL-23/IL-17 axis (IL23R, IL12B, STAT3) strongly associate with AS in genome-wide studies, providing the rationale for IL-17 and IL-23 inhibitor therapies.

Mechanical stress at entheses (ligament/tendon insertion points) combined with alterations in gut microbiome (subclinical ileocolitis in 50-60% of AS patients) are thought to trigger and sustain entheseal inflammation in genetically predisposed individuals. The 'functional enthesitis' hypothesis holds that resident entheseal macrophages and innate immune cells respond abnormally to mechanical loading in HLA-B27 carriers.

The IL-23/Th17 axis is central to AS pathogenesis. IL-23 drives differentiation of Th17 and innate lymphoid cells producing IL-17A; IL-17A in turn drives osteoblast activation and new bone formation at entheses, explaining the characteristic paradox of joint inflammation alongside pathological bone growth (syndesmophyte formation).

TNF-alpha is highly expressed at sacroiliac joints and spinal facets in active AS, driving synovial and entheseal inflammation. TNF is the most established therapeutic target — five TNF inhibitors (etanercept, adalimumab, infliximab, golimumab, certolizumab) are approved for AS.

riskFactors.description: Present in 90-95% of European AS patients. A HLA-B27-positive individual has approximately 1-5% lifetime risk of developing AS. When HLA-B27 is combined with a first-degree relative with AS, the risk rises to 15-20%.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of AS or axSpA
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of AS patients carry a 10-fold elevated risk above the general population baseline of 0.1-0.5%. Sibling recurrence risk is approximately 8-10% in HLA-B27-positive siblings.

Men are diagnosed 2-3× more frequently than women. Women are equally affected by the disease biologically but experience a milder phenotype with fewer radiographic changes, contributing to diagnostic delays averaging 7-12 years in women versus 5-8 years in men.
Symptom onset almost invariably before age 45; the peak is ages 20-30 years. Inflammatory back pain beginning after age 45 should prompt alternative diagnoses.

riskFactors.factor: Gut microbiome dysbiosis and subclinical ileitis
riskFactors.description: Subclinical gut inflammation (detectable only on ileocolonoscopy) occurs in approximately 50-60% of AS patients and correlates with disease activity. Enteric bacterial antigens (Klebsiella, Bacteroides) have been proposed as environmental triggers in HLA-B27-positive individuals.
Acute anterior uveitis without explained aetiology in an HLA-B27-positive young adult predicts development of axSpA in up to 30% of cases within 5 years.

affectedDemographics: Young men aged 20-35: the highest incidence group; AS is one of the most common causes of chronic back pain in this demographic.
Women: equally prevalent biologically but with milder radiographic changes and more peripheral joint and entheseal involvement, resulting in average diagnostic delays of 7-12 years.

HLA-B27 frequency varies by ethnicity: highest in circumpolar Inuit populations (25-40%), contributing to high AS rates; lowest in sub-Saharan Africa (<1% HLA-B27 frequency), explaining lower AS prevalence.
Patients with inflammatory bowel disease: 5-10% of Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis patients develop axial spondyloarthritis; reciprocally, 5-10% of AS patients have IBD.
diagnosisOverview: Ankylosing spondylitis is diagnosed by applying the 1984 modified New York criteria: chronic back pain (>3 months, onset before age 45, inflammatory pattern), combined with definite radiographic sacroiliitis (bilateral grade ≥2 or unilateral grade ≥3-4 on plain X-ray). When clinical suspicion is high but sacroiliitis is not evident on X-ray, MRI of the sacroiliac joints is performed — active sacroiliitis on STIR sequences (bone marrow oedema equivalent to ≥2 lesions per 2 consecutive slices) fulfils the ASAS 2009 imaging arm for nr-axSpA classification. HLA-B27 testing is a key supporting tool: a negative HLA-B27 substantially reduces the probability of AS. In clinical practice, the ASAS inflammatory back pain criteria (≥4 of 5 features: age of onset <40, insidious onset, improvement with exercise, no improvement with rest, nocturnal pain with improvement on arising) have 78.5% sensitivity and 72.4% specificity for identifying inflammatory back pain. Sacroiliac joint X-ray and MRI are the primary imaging tools; plain radiography of the lumbar spine looking for syndesmophytes and 'squaring' of vertebral bodies (Romanus lesion) confirms advanced structural disease. Laboratory tests (CRP, ESR, FBC) support disease activity assessment; elevated CRP is present in only 50-60% of AS patients with active disease. The BASDAI (Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Index) is the standard patient-reported outcome measure for disease activity assessment and biologic treatment eligibility (BASDAI ≥4 required by many national guidelines).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports diagnosis — positive in 90-95% of European AS; negative result substantially reduces pre-test probability in patients with inflammatory back pain
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw analysed by flow cytometry or molecular typing; results within 3-7 working days. A positive result alone does not diagnose AS — 8% of HLA-B27-positive people in the general population never develop AS.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates structural sacroiliitis required for modified New York criteria diagnosis; shows sclerosis, erosions, and eventual fusion of the SI joints
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A single plain X-ray of the pelvis, 10 minutes. Mild radiation. Sacroiliitis on X-ray takes an average 7-10 years from symptom onset to develop, so may be negative early in disease.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of the sacroiliac joints (STIR and T1 sequences)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects active (bone marrow oedema, synovitis) and structural (erosions, fat lesions, ankylosis) SI joint changes; enables earlier diagnosis than X-ray and fulfils ASAS nr-axSpA criteria
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 20-30 minute MRI without contrast typically; STIR sequences detect acute bone oedema. Requires experienced musculoskeletal radiologist interpretation. No radiation.

diagnosticTests.test: BASDAI (Bath AS Disease Activity Index) and ASDAS
diagnosticTests.purpose: Patient-reported and composite disease activity scores used to guide and evaluate biologic treatment eligibility and response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: BASDAI: 6-question self-report questionnaire scored 0-10; ≥4 indicates active disease. ASDAS: incorporates CRP or ESR with patient-reported fatigue, spinal pain, and stiffness. Takes 5 minutes to complete.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Markers of systemic inflammation; elevated in 50-60% of patients with active AS; normal values do not exclude active disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results same day. CRP correlates modestly with MRI sacroiliac bone marrow oedema and predicts structural progression over time.

diagnosticTests.test: Spine and pelvis X-ray (lateral lumbar and cervical)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies syndesmophytes (bridging ossification between vertebral bodies), 'squaring', and ankylosis in established AS; required for modified Stoke AS Spine Score (mSASSS) measurement in clinical trials
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard lateral X-rays of the lumbar and cervical spine; 10 minutes. Structural damage assessed annually to biannually in patients on biologic therapy.

treatmentOverview: AS treatment follows the 2022 ASAS-EULAR update and ACR 2019 guideline, both of which recommend NSAIDs as first-line pharmacological treatment for active AS, given continuously (rather than on-demand) because continuous NSAID use is associated with slower radiographic progression than intermittent use in randomised trials. Local corticosteroid injections (sacroiliac, hip, shoulder) are used adjunctively for peripheral joint or SI joint inflammation, though systemic glucocorticoids are not recommended for axial disease. Physiotherapy combining hydrotherapy, spinal extension exercises, and deep breathing is mandatory across all disease stages because maintaining spinal mobility delays progressive ankylosis. If adequate NSAID treatment (full dose for at least 4 weeks each) fails and BASDAI is ≥4, biologic therapy is indicated. Five TNF inhibitors (etanercept, adalimumab, infliximab, golimumab, certolizumab pegol) and three IL-17A inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab, bimekizumab) have evidence for AS. IL-17 inhibitors are preferred when psoriasis or IBD co-exists; TNF inhibitors are preferred for IBD. JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib, upadacitinib, filgotinib) are approved in several countries as second-line when the first biologic fails. Oral sulfasalazine has a modest benefit for peripheral joint disease only and is not used for axial symptoms. Spinal surgery is rarely needed: hip replacement for advanced hip arthritis and corrective osteotomy for severe kyphotic deformity improve function in carefully selected patients.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs — naproxen (500-1,000 mg/day), indomethacin (75-200 mg/day), etoricoxib (60-90 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for all symptomatic AS. Continuous use at full therapeutic dose achieves better pain control and slower radiographic progression than on-demand use. Protect the gastric mucosa with a PPI in patients at GI risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-70% of AS patients achieve clinically meaningful symptom relief; continuous NSAID use associated with significantly reduced syndesmophyte formation in the ENRICO RCT (Poddubnyy et al. 2012)

medicalTreatments.name: Adalimumab (40 mg SC every 2 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-TNF monoclonal antibody; one of five TNF inhibitors approved for AS. Reduces BASDAI, CRP, and MRI sacroiliac inflammation and slows radiographic progression. Screen for latent TB and hepatitis B before initiation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ASAS40 response (40% symptom improvement) in 45-58% vs 13-18% placebo in pivotal RCTs

medicalTreatments.name: Secukinumab (150 mg SC monthly after loading)
medicalTreatments.description: IL-17A inhibitor; first-line biologic choice when psoriasis co-exists or TNF failure. In active AS produces rapid and sustained ASAS40 improvement. Preferred over TNF inhibitors when psoriasis is a concurrent diagnosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ASAS40 response in 35-55% vs 10-15% placebo; equivalent to TNF inhibitors in head-to-head studies; superior for psoriatic skin involvement

medicalTreatments.name: Etanercept (50 mg SC weekly or 25 mg twice weekly)
medicalTreatments.description: TNF receptor-fusion protein; the original biologic approved for AS. Not effective for IBD (a key advantage of other TNF inhibitors). Useful in patients without gut comorbidities and for whom weekly dosing is preferred.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ASAS20 response (20% improvement) in 59-68% vs 22-28% placebo; well-established long-term safety data

medicalTreatments.name: Upadacitinib (15 mg once daily orally)
medicalTreatments.description: Selective JAK1 inhibitor; approved for AS in patients with inadequate response to at least one biologic. Oral administration. Requires screening for TB, hepatitis, and thromboembolism risk before initiation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ASAS40 response in 45% vs 18% placebo in SELECT-AXIS 2; non-inferior to adalimumab in head-to-head trial

medicalTreatments.name: Sulfasalazine (2-3 g/day in divided doses)
medicalTreatments.description: Conventional DMARD with modest efficacy specifically for peripheral joint involvement (oligoarthritis, enthesitis) in spondyloarthritis; no evidence for axial disease. Alternative when biologics are not available or affordable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Peripheral joint benefit in 40-50%; no radiographic benefit for axial disease confirmed in multiple trials

surgicalOptions.name: Total hip replacement (THA)
surgicalOptions.description: Indicated for severe hip arthritis with functional limitation and pain despite maximal medical therapy. Hip involvement in AS carries worse functional outcomes than hip OA if untreated; 20-35% of AS patients require THA within 30 years of diagnosis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Pain and function improvement in over 90% of patients; implant 15-year survival approximately 85-90% in AS patients; perioperative heterotopic ossification prophylaxis (indomethacin or irradiation) recommended

surgicalOptions.name: Corrective spinal osteotomy (pedicle subtraction osteotomy)
surgicalOptions.description: Performed for severe fixed kyphotic deformity (chin-brow vertical angle >35°) when the patient cannot achieve horizontal gaze or stand erect. Major procedure with significant neurological risk; reserved for patients with severe deformity after failure of all medical options.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Sagittal balance restoration in 85-95% of carefully selected patients; neurological complication rate 3-10%

alternativeTreatments.name: Hydrotherapy and land-based physiotherapy (ASAS/EULAR recommended)
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured extension exercises, swimming, and postural training are EULAR-recommended non-pharmacological interventions with Level 1a evidence. A 2007 Cochrane review (Dagfinrud et al.) found that supervised exercise programmes significantly improved spinal mobility, chest expansion, and patient-reported outcomes compared with no exercise.

alternativeTreatments.name: TNF inhibitor-combined exercise programmes
alternativeTreatments.description: A 2012 RCT showed that adding an intensive 3-week inpatient exercise programme to TNF inhibitor treatment produced significantly greater BASDAI and BASFI (functional index) improvement than TNF inhibitor alone at 6 months, underscoring the necessity of combining pharmacotherapy with physiotherapy.

whySeeSpecialist: Rheumatologist assessment is essential to confirm the diagnosis of AS, exclude alternative causes of inflammatory back pain, initiate and monitor biologic therapy, and screen for extra-articular manifestations. The average diagnostic delay is 7-10 years in AS, which can be reduced to under 2 years with specialist-led assessment triggered by inflammatory back pain criteria.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a rheumatologist with a subspecialty interest in spondyloarthritis, access to MRI for sacroiliac joint imaging, and experience prescribing and monitoring TNF and IL-17 inhibitors. Shared care with physiotherapy, ophthalmology, and gastroenterology is essential for patients with extra-articular features.
hospitalCriteria: Acute neurological deficit from spinal fracture through fused segments — requires emergency CT, spine surgery assessment
Acute anterior uveitis not responding to topical steroids within 48-72 hours
First biologic infusion (infliximab) requiring inpatient monitoring for infusion reactions
Pre-surgical assessment for total hip replacement or corrective osteotomy
Rapidly progressive respiratory failure from severe chest-wall restriction or apical pulmonary fibrosis
keyFacilities: Specialist spondyloarthritis clinics within academic rheumatology departments
Physiotherapy departments with AS-specific exercise programmes and hydrotherapy
Orthopaedic units experienced in total hip replacement for ankylosing spondylitis
Ophthalmology outpatient service for uveitis management
preventionStrategies: Early diagnosis reduces the window for irreversible structural damage — young adults with inflammatory back pain (nocturnal, improving with exercise) should be assessed by a rheumatologist within 6-12 weeks rather than managed as mechanical back pain for years.
Continuous NSAID therapy in patients with active AS slows syndesmophyte formation; intermittent as-needed NSAID use does not share this structural benefit and should be avoided in active disease.
HLA-B27-positive individuals with a first-degree relative with AS should have back pain investigated promptly, as the familial risk is 10-fold above baseline.
Maintain HLA-B27-positive individuals in regular physical activity — prevention of sedentary behaviour reduces the risk of rapid ankylosis and maintains muscle strength supporting spinal posture.
Screen AS patients for acute anterior uveitis at every review — prompt treatment of uveitis with dilating drops and topical steroids prevents the most common cause of preventable vision loss in AS.
lifestyleModifications: Perform daily AS-specific extension exercises (prone lying, thoracic extension over a rolled towel, deep breathing, shoulder retraction) for at least 20-30 minutes — this is the single most important self-management intervention for maintaining spinal mobility.
Swimming and hydrotherapy are the exercise modes with the strongest EULAR recommendation for AS — the buoyancy reduces load on inflamed joints while enabling full spinal mobility training.
Sleep on a firm mattress without a pillow or with minimal cervical support to maintain the spine in extension rather than flexion.
Stop smoking — smoking is independently associated with worse radiographic progression, higher inflammatory markers, and poorer quality of life in AS across multiple cohort studies.
Maintain a healthy weight to reduce mechanical load on hips, which are the most commonly damaged peripheral joints in AS.

Inform a dentist or anaesthetist about AS before any procedure requiring dental opening or neck extension — cervical ankylosis limits mouth opening and cervical mobility, requiring fibre-optic intubation.
Attend annual eye screening with an ophthalmologist for uveitis surveillance, even in the absence of acute symptoms.

recommended: Anti-inflammatory diet pattern (Mediterranean diet — vegetables, fish, olive oil, whole grains) — epidemiological data associate adherence with lower CRP and improved quality of life in inflammatory arthritis
Oily fish and omega-3 supplements (EPA+DHA 2-4 g/day) — reduce systemic inflammation; evidence from RA and axSpA cohort studies
Calcium-rich foods and vitamin D supplementation — reduce glucocorticoid-related and immobility-related bone loss; fracture risk is elevated in AS
Probiotic-rich foods (fermented vegetables, yoghurt) — the gut microbiome modulates AS activity; preliminary trial data support microbiome-directed interventions
avoid: Excessive alcohol — worsens NSAID and methotrexate hepatotoxicity and reduces exercise adherence
High-calorie, ultra-processed foods — obesity worsens hip and knee entheseal stress, impairs biologic response, and increases cardiovascular risk from chronic inflammation
Smoking — independently associated with worse structural progression and poorer response to anti-TNF therapy in multiple cohort studies
Excessive calcium from supplements without vitamin D — in patients on NSAIDs does not reduce GI risk and may worsen constipation

exerciseGuidelines: Daily physical activity is an EULAR Grade A recommendation for AS. Extension-dominant exercises (prone lying, swimming, rowing) counteract the flexion bias of advancing kyphosis. Aerobic exercise 3-5 times per week is additionally beneficial for cardiovascular risk reduction — the chronic inflammatory state in AS increases cardiovascular risk 1.3-1.5-fold. High-contact sports or those with fall/collision risk should be avoided in patients with established spinal fusion due to fracture risk.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed NSAID with food at the same time each day; continuous rather than as-needed NSAID use is recommended in active disease.
Complete the morning exercise routine before the day's activities — morning stiffness is worst on arising and exercise is most effective at this time.
Inject biologic agents (adalimumab, secukinumab, etanercept) on the scheduled injection day without delay — missed doses risk disease flare and anti-drug antibody formation.
Check eyes for any redness, pain, or photophobia — anterior uveitis is the most common emergency in AS and requires ophthalmology review within 24 hours of onset.
Monitor bowel symptoms; new bloody diarrhoea or abdominal cramping in an AS patient on NSAIDs or biologics warrants gastroenterology review to assess for new or worsening IBD.
Attend BASDAI and CRP review at least every 6 months on biologic therapy to assess continued efficacy and justify continuation.
prognosis: The long-term outlook for AS has improved markedly since the introduction of TNF inhibitors. With early diagnosis and appropriate treatment, most patients achieve sustained low disease activity or remission and maintain functional independence. Without treatment, 20-30% of patients develop significant spinal ankylosis over 20 years, with the 'bamboo spine' deformity causing major disability. The modified Stoke AS Spine Score (mSASSS) progression averages 1-2 units per year on NSAIDs alone and under 0.5 units per year with continuous biologic therapy in trials. Hip involvement (present in 20-35% of patients) and early disease onset are the strongest predictors of long-term functional impairment. Cardiovascular mortality is increased approximately 1.3-1.5-fold in AS compared with the general population, driven by chronic inflammation, reduced physical activity, and higher rates of smoking. Overall mortality is not substantially elevated in well-managed AS, but the physical burden of ankylosis, uveitis, and functional disability significantly reduces quality of life in inadequately treated disease.
recoveryTimeline: NSAIDs produce clinically meaningful pain and stiffness reduction within 48-72 hours of initiation in active AS — a rapid early response to full-dose NSAIDs is itself diagnostically informative. TNF or IL-17 inhibitors produce their primary response within 12 weeks; ASAS40 response rates plateau at 24-52 weeks. Physiotherapy benefits accrue over 3-6 months of consistent practice. Structural damage accumulation is very slow and measurable only over years.
complications: Spinal ankylosis with bamboo spine — 20-30% of patients over 20 years without adequate therapy; causes fixed kyphosis and severe restriction of movement, driving occupational disability.
Acute anterior uveitis — occurs in 25-40% of AS patients; recurrent attacks can cause synechiae, cataracts, glaucoma, and permanent visual impairment if untreated.
Vertebral fractures through fused spinal segments — AS bone is brittle within the fused region; minor trauma causes unstable fractures with high spinal cord injury risk.
Hip joint destruction requiring arthroplasty — occurs in 20-35% of AS patients; is the most common cause of permanent disability beyond spinal fusion.
Cardiovascular disease — ischaemic heart disease and stroke risk approximately 1.3-1.5-fold elevated; aortic regurgitation and conduction defects occur in longstanding AS.
Apical pulmonary fibrosis — rare late complication of AS; causes progressive dyspnoea and predisposes to cavitatory infection with Aspergillus.

supportResources.name: National Axial Spondyloarthritis Society (NASS)
supportResources.description: UK charity providing exercise programmes, helplines, peer support, and advocacy for people with AS and nr-axSpA.

supportResources.name: Spondylitis Association of America (SAA)
supportResources.description: US patient organisation with AS education, patient stories, clinical trial registries, and a biologic decision guide.

supportResources.name: ASAS-EULAR 2022 Recommendations for the Management of Axial Spondyloarthritis
supportResources.description: The current international management guideline; freely accessible; covers NSAIDs, biologics, and JAK inhibitors.

supportResources.name: ACR 2019 Guideline for the Treatment of Ankylosing Spondylitis and Nonradiographic Axial Spondyloarthritis
supportResources.description: US guideline with evidence ratings for all pharmacological and non-pharmacological interventions.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriatic Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriatic arthritis (PsA) belongs to the spondyloarthritis family and can involve the sacroiliac joints and spine (axial PsA), but is distinguished by the presence of psoriatic skin or nail disease, asymmetric sacroiliitis (often unilateral), dactylitis, and a pattern of asymmetric peripheral joint involvement. HLA-B27 is positive in only 20-50% of PsA versus 90-95% of AS.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: RA causes symmetric small-joint polyarthritis, elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies, and erosive joint damage — the pattern is the opposite of AS, which primarily affects large joints and the spine asymmetrically, and is rheumatoid factor negative. Morning stiffness is present in both but the joint distribution differs fundamentally.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Fibromyalgia causes widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and sleep disruption without objective joint inflammation, elevated CRP, or sacroiliac MRI changes. The pain of fibromyalgia does not specifically improve with exercise and movement, does not cause structural damage, and is not HLA-B27-associated. The two conditions can co-exist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Osteoarthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Degenerative lumbar spondylosis and facet joint OA cause chronic low back pain but are typically worse with activity and improve with rest — the opposite of inflammatory back pain in AS. OA affects older adults, lacks sacroiliitis, is HLA-B27 negative, and does not respond to NSAIDs as dramatically as AS in the first 48-72 hours.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriatic Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are spondyloarthritis conditions sharing HLA-B27 association, enthesitis, and response to TNF and IL-17 inhibitors; they are managed by the same specialist team.

relatedConditions.name: Reactive Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another spondyloarthritis characterised by joint inflammation following enteric or genitourinary infection; shares HLA-B27 association and may evolve into axSpA.

relatedConditions.name: ANCA-Associated Vasculitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another autoimmune rheumatological disease requiring specialist immunosuppressive management; occasionally co-occurs with spondyloarthritis in clinical practice.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common inflammatory arthritis managed in the same rheumatology clinic as AS; shares biologic treatment classes but with distinct mechanisms and joint patterns.

faqs.question: What is ankylosing spondylitis in simple terms?
faqs.answer: Ankylosing spondylitis is a chronic inflammatory arthritis primarily affecting the sacroiliac joints and spine. The hallmark is back pain that worsens with rest and improves with movement. Over years without treatment, the spine can fuse, causing a fixed forward stoop. It is strongly linked to the HLA-B27 gene and is treated with anti-inflammatory drugs and biologics.

faqs.question: How is ankylosing spondylitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis uses the modified New York criteria: inflammatory back pain plus definite sacroiliitis on X-ray, or sacroiliac bone marrow oedema on MRI for the nr-axSpA classification. HLA-B27 testing supports diagnosis. The BASDAI questionnaire quantifies disease activity. Average diagnostic delay is 7-10 years from symptom onset.

faqs.question: What is HLA-B27 and does it mean you have AS?
faqs.answer: HLA-B27 is a genetic marker present in 90-95% of European AS patients. A positive HLA-B27 alone does not diagnose AS — 8% of the general population carries it without developing AS. The test supports diagnosis when combined with inflammatory back pain and imaging changes. Negative HLA-B27 substantially reduces the probability of AS.

faqs.question: Can ankylosing spondylitis be cured?
faqs.answer: AS cannot be cured, but modern biologics (TNF and IL-17 inhibitors) achieve sustained remission or low disease activity in the majority of treated patients and significantly slow structural progression. Physical therapy is equally important for maintaining mobility. Early diagnosis and continuous NSAID use before biologic therapy further reduce long-term damage.

faqs.question: What exercises help ankylosing spondylitis?
faqs.answer: Extension-dominant exercises — swimming, prone lying, thoracic extension over a rolled towel, chest expansion, and deep breathing — are recommended daily. Supervised physiotherapy and hydrotherapy programmes consistently improve spinal mobility and pain in clinical trials. Exercise is the most important non-drug intervention for AS and cannot be substituted by rest.

faqs.question: What are the best medications for ankylosing spondylitis?
faqs.answer: NSAIDs (naproxen, indomethacin, etoricoxib) taken continuously are first-line. When NSAIDs fail (BASDAI ≥4), biologics are used: five TNF inhibitors (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab, golimumab, certolizumab) and three IL-17A inhibitors (secukinumab, ixekizumab, bimekizumab) are approved. JAK inhibitors (upadacitinib) are second-line biologic options.

faqs.question: What is bamboo spine?
faqs.answer: Bamboo spine is the X-ray appearance of advanced ankylosing spondylitis where syndesmophytes (bony outgrowths) bridge all vertebral bodies, fusing the spine into a rigid column resembling bamboo. It develops in 20-30% of untreated patients over 20 years and is associated with severe mobility restriction and high fracture risk.

faqs.question: Can women get ankylosing spondylitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Women are equally affected biologically by AS, but are diagnosed 2-3× less often because their radiographic changes are milder and more peripheral. Women experience an average diagnostic delay of 7-12 years versus 5-8 years in men. Women with AS more commonly present with peripheral joint symptoms, enthesitis, and fatigue.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ankylosing spondylitis and axial spondyloarthritis?
faqs.answer: Axial spondyloarthritis (axSpA) is the umbrella term covering the full spectrum from non-radiographic axSpA (MRI evidence of sacroiliitis only) to radiographic axSpA (definite X-ray sacroiliitis), the latter being what was historically called ankylosing spondylitis. Both are treated identically and represent the same disease at different structural stages.

faqs.question: Does ankylosing spondylitis affect the eyes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Acute anterior uveitis — sudden unilateral red eye, photophobia, and blurred vision — occurs in 25-40% of AS patients, making it the most common extra-articular feature. It is treated with topical corticosteroid eye drops and cycloplegic agents. Repeated attacks can cause permanent visual damage if not treated promptly.

faqs.question: How does ankylosing spondylitis affect the heart?
faqs.answer: Long-standing AS can cause aortic root inflammation, aortic regurgitation, and cardiac conduction defects in 5-10% of patients. The chronic inflammatory state also increases cardiovascular risk (ischaemic heart disease and stroke) by approximately 1.3-1.5-fold. Regular cardiovascular risk factor monitoring is recommended.

faqs.question: Is ankylosing spondylitis hereditary?
faqs.answer: AS has a strong genetic component with heritability estimated at 80-90%. HLA-B27 is the major risk gene. First-degree relatives carry approximately 10-fold elevated risk above the general population. Having a parent with AS and being HLA-B27 positive confers approximately 15-20% lifetime risk of developing AS.

faqs.question: What is the BASDAI score in ankylosing spondylitis?
faqs.answer: The BASDAI (Bath Ankylosing Spondylitis Disease Activity Index) is a patient-completed 6-question tool scoring fatigue, spinal and peripheral joint pain, enthesitis, and morning stiffness on a 0-10 scale. A BASDAI ≥4 despite adequate NSAID therapy indicates active disease and is the key threshold used by most national guidelines to justify biologic prescribing.

faqs.question: Does ankylosing spondylitis get worse over time?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, 20-30% of patients develop significant spinal fusion over 20 years. With NSAIDs and biologics, structural progression can be substantially reduced. Smoking, high baseline CRP, and male sex predict faster radiographic progression. Many patients with early diagnosis and consistent treatment maintain normal or near-normal function long-term.

faqs.question: Can ankylosing spondylitis cause spinal fractures?
faqs.answer: Yes. Once the spine is fused, even minor trauma can cause fractures through the brittle, stiffened segments. Cervical spine fractures are particularly dangerous and can cause quadriplegia. Any new or worsening back pain after trauma in a known AS patient requires emergency CT of the whole spine rather than X-ray alone.

simpleName: Ankylosing spondylitis (AS, bamboo spine, axial spondyloarthritis)
metaDescription: Ankylosing spondylitis: HLA-B27-linked spine arthritis causing inflammatory back pain and progressive fusion. Symptoms, biologics and exercises reviewed.
